children.name: Colinus
children.description: Small, plump quail with mottled brown plumage and a distinctive white or buff facial stripe. They are highly social, forming coveys that forage together on the ground.

children.name: Colinus virginianus
children.description: One of the most familiar quail of eastern and central North America, named for its clear whistled 'bob-white' call. It inhabits open woodlands, fields and agricultural edges, where coveys of 10 to 20 birds roost in tight circles on the ground. Its populations have declined significantly due to habitat loss and fire suppression.

children.name: Callipepla
children.description: Elegant quail with a distinctive forward-curving plume of feathers on the crown, composed of six upright feathers. They are adapted to arid and semi-arid landscapes of western North America.

children.name: Callipepla californica
children.description: California's state bird, easily recognised by the dangling teardrop-shaped plume curving forward from the forehead and the scaled black-and-white pattern on the belly. It is a common resident of chaparral, oak woodlands and suburban parks from southern Oregon to Baja California. Introduced populations now thrive in New Zealand and Chile.

children.name: Callipepla gambelii
children.description: A handsome desert-dwelling quail with a chestnut crown, black face and a striking teardrop plume that curves forward. Found in the arid scrublands of the southwestern United States and northwestern Mexico, it forms large coveys that scurry through creosote bush and mesquite. It can survive without standing water by obtaining moisture from succulent vegetation.

children.name: Callipepla squamata
children.description: Pale grey-brown quail with a distinctive scaly pattern on the breast and upper belly, and a short, whitish crest. It occupies the arid grasslands and scrublands of the south-central United States and northern Mexico. Known locally as 'blue quail' for its bluish-grey plumage, it is more shy and harder to flush than its relatives.

children.name: Oreortyx
children.description: A monotypic genus of the largest New World quail, characterised by a long, straight black plume rising vertically from the crown. It is an expert runner and prefers rugged, mountainous terrain.

children.name: Oreortyx pictus
children.description: The largest New World quail, recognisable by its long, straight black head plume and rich chestnut, blue-grey and white plumage. It inhabits dense chaparral and coniferous forests of the Pacific mountain ranges from Washington to Baja California. Unlike other quail, it prefers steep slopes and is more likely to run than fly when threatened.

children.name: Cyrtonyx
children.description: Distinctive, stocky quail with an extremely short tail and an intricately patterned plumage of black, white and chestnut. They are among the most beautiful but secretive of the New World quail.

children.name: Cyrtonyx montezumae
children.description: Named for the Aztec emperor, this strikingly patterned quail has a face marked like a harlequin with bold white, black and chestnut swirls. It ranges from the southwestern United States through the Sierra Madre to central Mexico. It feeds almost exclusively on underground tubers and bulbs, which it excavates with powerful scratching motions.
